file_name: Kate et al. - 2025 - Explainable AI framework for precise and trustworthy skin cancer diagnosis.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning architectures (VGG16, InceptionV3, Inception-ResNet V2, and DenseNet-201) fine-tuned on the HAM10000 dataset to classify skin lesions as benign or malignant. It also employs Explainable AI (XAI) methods including LIME, SHAP, and Grad-CAM to interpret the model's predictions for skin cancer diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a new Explainable AI framework for skin cancer diagnosis. It details the methodology of fine-tuning specific deep learning architectures (VGG16, InceptionV3, Inception-ResNet V2, DenseNet-201) on the HAM10000 dataset, implements XAI techniques (LIME, SHAP, Grad-CAM), and reports original results including IoU scores and performance comparisons. Although published in conference proceedings (INCOFT 2025), it contains original experimental results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Explainable AI Framework for Precise and Trustworthy Skin Cancer Diagnosis
journal: Proceedings of the 3rd International Conference on Futuristic Technology (INCOFT 2025)
brief_summary: This study proposes an Explainable AI (XAI) framework to enhance the precision and trustworthiness of skin cancer diagnosis by addressing the limitations of invasive biopsies and subjective dermoscopy. The researchers fine-tuned four deep learning architectures—VGG16, InceptionV3, Inception-ResNet V2, and DenseNet-201—on the HAM10000 benchmark dataset to classify skin lesions as benign or malignant. To ensure transparency, the framework integrates LIME, SHAP, and Grad-CAM techniques to visualize key image features and regions influencing model decisions. The evaluation approach involves comparing these pre-trained models and analyzing their interpretability through layer-wise analysis and heatmaps. The results demonstrate that integrating XAI methods helps highlight discriminative areas, thereby improving model reliability and user trust in clinical decision support systems.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion categories (benign vs malignant, or specific classes from HAM10000 dataset)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the model is used to 'distinguish skin lesions as benign or malignant' and to 'classify skin lesions'. It utilizes the HAM10000 dataset which contains seven classes (Melanocytic nevi, Melanoma, Benign keratosis similar lesions, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratoses, Vascular lesions, and Dermatofibroma). The task is described as 'skin lesion classification' and 'binary classification' in the abstract and keywords.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset containing more than 10,000 dermatoscopic images of skin lesions, used for diagnosing and categorizing skin cancer. It is noted for having high class imbalance.

use.training: Fine-tuned pre-trained deep learning models (VGG16, Inception V3, Inception ResNet V2, DenseNet 201) on this dataset for skin lesion classification.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (referenced in Figure 2: Model Training Performance).
use.other: Used as the primary data source for the proposed Explainable AI framework.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The study focuses on comparing model performance metrics (accuracy, loss, IoU) and visualizing explanations using XAI techniques (LIME, SHAP, Grad-CAM) without statistical significance testing.

VGG16.notes: Performance illustrated in Figure 2 (Model Training Performance) but specific numerical values not reported in text.

Inception V3.notes: Performance illustrated in Figure 2 (Model Training Performance) but specific numerical values not reported in text.

Inception ResNet V2.notes: Performance illustrated in Figure 2 (Model Training Performance) but specific numerical values not reported in text.

DenseNet 201.notes: Performance illustrated in Figure 2 (Model Training Performance) but specific numerical values not reported in text.

Baseline CNN.notes: Used as a benchmark for comparison; specific numerical values not reported in text.

HAM10000.notes: The paper reports that Figure 2 illustrates training and validation accuracy and loss graphs for the four fine-tuned models. It mentions evaluating performance using Interpretation over Union (IoU) scores (Figure 6) to assess XAI methods (LIME, SHAP, Grad-CAM). Specific quantitative IoU values or accuracy percentages are not provided in the text chunks.

architecture_used.main_model: Four fine-tuned pre-trained deep learning architectures: VGG16, Inception V3, Inception-ResNet V2, and DenseNet 201. A baseline CNN with three convolution and pooling layers was also used for comparison.
architecture_used.pretraining: The four main models were initially trained on ImageNet to learn generic image features before being fine-tuned on the HAM10000 dataset for skin lesion classification.

architecture_details.VGG16: Composed of five convolutional blocks, where the third, fourth, and fifth blocks have four convolutional layers each.
architecture_details.Inception V3: Utilizes 'Inception modules' for efficient feature extraction; described as computationally efficient.
architecture_details.Inception ResNet V2: Based on Inception V3, combines residual connections to enable deeper network training, and minimizes issues of Batch Normalization.
architecture_details.DenseNet 201: Comprises four 'dense blocks' where each block generates feature maps through batch normalization, ReLU, and 3x3 convolution. Transition layers between dense blocks consist of convolution and pooling layers.
architecture_details.Baseline CNN: Basic structure with only three convolution and pooling layers.

training_details.method: Fine-tuning of pre-trained models on the HAM10000 dataset.

classification_or_output_head.details: The models were fine-tuned to distinguish skin lesions as benign or malignant. The paper mentions the use of Global Average Pooling (GAP) in the context of CAM methods to preserve spatial structures and detect discriminative areas, which improves decision-making.

preprocessing_techniques: Image segmentation into superpixels for LIME analysis (Step 1: Segment Image, Divide I into k superpixels)
Generation of perturbed images by masking superpixels for LIME analysis (Step 2: Generate Perturbed Images, Create perturbed images by masking superpixels)
conclusions: The proposed Explainable AI (XAI) framework integrating VGG16, InceptionV3, Inception-ResNet V2, and DenseNet-201 fine-tuned on the HAM10000 dataset effectively distinguishes skin lesions as benign or malignant.

Grad-CAM demonstrated superior performance compared to LIME and SHAP in terms of Interpretation over Union (IoU) scores, indicating its ability to detect medically important zones and class-discriminative areas more accurately.
Grad-CAM is particularly useful for healthcare applications due to its high visual interpretability and ability to highlight regions that influenced the model's predictions.
LIME and SHAP did not focus on regions similar to ground truths as effectively as Grad-CAM in this study.
The integration of XAI methods improves model reliability and user trust by providing transparency into how deep learning models make decisions in skin cancer diagnosis.
Future work should validate these findings by evaluating emerging architectures such as Vision Transformers (ViTs) on more heterogeneous samples of medical imaging data.
Applying domain knowledge and user feedback holds potential for enhancing the usefulness of explainability methods in critical medical applications.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is noted for having high class imbalance, potentially limiting the generalizability of the results to other populations or datasets.
Specific quantitative performance metrics (accuracy, loss, IoU scores) are not reported in the text; results are only illustrated graphically in Figure 2 and Figure 6 without numerical values.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported to support the comparative claims between models or XAI methods.
The paper does not report external validation; the models were fine-tuned and evaluated on the HAM10000 benchmark dataset only.

The authors acknowledge that LIME and SHAP did not focus on regions similar to ground truths as effectively as Grad-CAM in this specific study, suggesting potential limitations in feature attribution for these methods in this context.
The paper is a conference proceeding (INCOFT 2025) rather than a peer-reviewed journal article, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.
The study focuses on binary classification (benign vs. malignant) despite the HAM10000 dataset containing seven classes, potentially oversimplifying the clinical diagnostic task.
